video_title: Why Smart Investors Still Make Costly Mistakes
transcript: You can't make a decision without emotion. Understand your impulsivity first before you you trust your intuition. Performance equals potential minus interferences. This is really what mental performance training is. And that's why the best stay the best. >> So you are performance coach for traders and money managers, but also sort of a lot of just high-level perform performers, right? Executives, athletes, everyone has got to kind of operate at that I would say super stressful level, but maybe not. At least a very high high level. So so it's going to be great to get your insight across sort of decision-making. But what are some of the common mistakes that you see investors repeatedly make? >> So I live in the world of high decision-making under pressure. So whether that's athletes, global CEOs, C-suite, and obviously Wall Street professionals, or anybody who deals with high-pressure situations where decisions are essential. So at M1, you know, where we merge psychoanalytic theory and neuroscience. So basically marrying the why and the how. Why do we do what we do and how do we get better? So when we think about decision-making, it's you can't make a decision without emotion. But unfortunately for many people, emotions are tied to past behaviors. And so what happens at that that usually comes in the form of reacting. So how do they go how do I get the people I serve out of a reactionary stance into responding? And the interesting about this, Maggie and Richard, is that we know we can't make decisions without emotions. However, usually our emotions get onto the field and they start making decisions for us based on past experiences. That's the psychoanalytic part of that. From past, you know, from how we were programmed as children, what our beliefs are and and so on and so on. So, it's my job as a coach is to, in the face of any emotion, how do we create space to pick conscious behavior, which in turn, and this is real cool part, it starts to build new experiences in the brain, which then causes a change in your neural circuitry, which is which is very important, right? That's neuroplasticity. So, for me as a coach and and dealing with people who I say live in the left lane, who are just running and gunning, how do I slow down things without touching the gas in order to speed them up on a decision basis? And that's what I do all day long. >> I think that's so interesting. First of all, change is possible, which is super in I think interesting and and wonderful to hear because I think sometimes we think we're locked into patterns and and we can't get out. So, it's amazing to hear that the brain can kind of rewire. Um and you said something really interesting, reacting versus responding. Um and and I have a feeling that's that's really important in this process. So, it's worth noting, so you you know of what you speak because you managed money for years. Uh and so you were in that in that left lane, fast lane, um having to react uh really quickly. What did you discover through your own experience um when it came to the connection between psychology and trading? Like, what was the what was the sort of aha moment for you that led you down this path? >> Well, the aha moment was I thought I had a heart attack at 46 and then ended my career, and it was a massive panic attack, and they realized the gray matter in my brain was significantly impaired through I was a D1 athlete through many concussions and chronic stress and and actually past trauma. Mhm. So, I was like, how is that possible? How at 46 I'm done? And I was very lucky to pivot. You had no idea that you were kind of that was happening to you. >> I You know what's interesting, Maggie? I don't want to I don't want to acknowledge it. >> Mhm. >> I mean, I had all these concussions. I was an athlete. And I knew I was dealing with chronic stress constantly, but nobody was talking about emotions. We had past coaches saying, "Just get bigger and do more work." And I was like, "Mhm. I'm doing that. But I keep repeating patterns over and over again. It's something called repetition compulsion. Why can't I stop doing this? >> Mhm. >> I know what to do. But why do I keep even though my environment may look different, I'm creating the same experience again and again and again? So then I was very fortunate to meet somebody and and they said, "Listen, you are repeating past patterns." I'm like, "What does that mean? I was I was a kid growing up in Long Island. How am I repeating?" And I was a D1 athlete, but I blew my knees out twice. So I had identity shift. And believe it or not, I was projecting onto the market of how I wanted to feel. >> Mhm. On to trading. On to your life. >> community that is a not a good thing. >> Mhm. That's so interesting. >> like I deserved success. I I Why? Why I don't understand what is going on in my head. And then I went down the rabbit hole. >> So I'm going to put a pin in deserving success cuz I think that's really important, too. So So you you So you go down the rabbit hole. Um it's Are we aware of what's happening when we're acting? I think we think we we we feel like we get to a point and and we're talking to people who are sophisticated investors. Many of them have accumulated a lot of wealth. So they've had success. Which I think is Maybe that's the Maybe that's the confusion, right? >> interesting. >> They've had success. They've done well. Uh are we aware of what's happening? And And so, there's a track record to to to reflect on. Are we aware of maybe why we're we're underperforming or when things do blow up or they don't go well, why it's happening? Do we have an awareness of that? >> So, I did a I did a a keynote at Gurney's for a conference. This is less less 2 months, 3 months. And it's a blind spot. So, we know we always hear we learn in struggle. >> Mhm. >> But, we never talk about how do we learn in success? Like, we can have success, but how do you stay successful? >> Mhm. >> So, we know now in this environment, like I haven't been trading now and so every once in a while I'll get the urge, right? When when things get a little chaotic, it's the trader in me that I have to participate. But, when you think about the context has changed, environments change. >> Mhm. >> So, that old saying, what got you here is not going to get you where you need to go. So, the blind the blind spot is really not acknowledging what is going on between your ears. And how do we get better? When you think about incredible athletes, incredible CEOs, incredible hedge fund managers, why do they all, and I'm not playing my book here, why do they all have coaches? >> Mhm. >> Because they realize unconsciously they may be repeating patterns that they are not aware of, thus the word unconscious. So, how do we become more conscious of the things that we're not aware of? It's a great question, and here's the answer. There's so many different techniques in mental performance to make yourself more visible, which means more conscious. And the first thing you do should you should ask yourself something called self-exploratory questions. And what is that? We know when we're in a meeting or an interview, the person asking the question is the one in the seat of influence. >> Mhm. >> They're kind of controlling the meeting. Self-exploratory Excuse me, self-exploratory questions is when we ask ourselves personal questions. What am I trying to accomplish here? What How am I feeling and why? Why am I reacting and not responding? So, when we ask ourselves these questions and and Maggie, it has to come out of your mouth. You just can't think this. It creates mental space for us to think about it. That alone right there takes you from reacting to responding. Right? So, when you think about trading and you think about investing, we all know what intuition is and we know what impulsivity is. Right? But, impulsivity is chaos. That's reacting. Intuition is calmness. That's responding. >> Mhm. >> So, we all think as we get older, I'm 54, that I'm very intuitive. I've seen all these things and so, when I do something, I can feel that reaction. But, I always say to people, understand your impulsivities first before you you trust your intuition. So, when you think about performance, a very simple very simple equation. Performance equals potential minus interferences. The better we are at at diminishing interferences, they don't go away completely, the more energy we have we the more energy we have of really creating more potential for ourselves. And then the equation shifts. Right? There goes performance. >> Mhm. >> So, when we're successful and we're doing well, we assume everything is going well. However, there are little kinks in the foundation and they're developing and we're not aware of them. So, in order to stay successful, we have to occasionally look under the hood and ask yourself, am Am >> Yeah. >> Is my engine oiled up enough that I'm moving the way I need to be moving? That's that's self-reflection. >> Yeah, and I think that people I think that people sometimes, you know, sort of blame it on the market, right? Like they had it right. >> But the mar right, Mr. Market sort of, you know, unexpectedly did something and and that sort of ruined the plan or it or sort of ran them over. >> This is great. This is great. So so you hit on a couple different things. So when you are navigating markets, navigating investments, you have to be extremely comfortable with uncertainty. But you also have to be extremely confident in your behavior. So when when you talk about people have plans, the reason we have plans is to create what? Create certainty. How can you create certainty out of uncertainty? Now, if you say I have a structure in the way I think, I want to be able to be flexible within that structure, not rigid. That's a different mindset. It doesn't let you off the hook of of of being impulsive, but it gives you that flexibility. Think about rigidity as as scarcity, as you get smaller. Flexibility, you get to open up and your brain actually gets much more clarity and gives you space to what? Respond to what's happening. >> Mhm. >> It's it's it's like one of those things it sounds so simple, but in anything we do, it has to be repetitive. So when you have high expectations, like I think I could do this, I've done this, I'm good at this, and you don't have the sufficient training, I'm 54. I've been coaching I'm still training. >> Mhm. >> When that gap gets big, Maggie, we've got a problem here. Cuz now we're making assumptions on expectations and we're really not working on ourselves. You know, they you've heard of meta-cognition. >> Mhm. How do I think about thinking? And by the way, this is this is the path of excellence. Not everybody has to do this. It takes a lot of work to have this set intention in the morning. Be reflective night be reflective at night and not ruminate. Right, rumination is a behavior. It's dwelling back in the past bad behaviors. Right, you've heard that line perception follows belief. Now you are filling in why you don't deserve it. As opposed to I've done this well, where do I need to get better? That's feed forward. This is constant. >> Yeah. >> If you're my client, they're probably exhausted hearing this. >> Yeah. >> This is a constant thing and why is that why is repetition important as we go through this? It starts to rebuild the brain. You know, as a kid I was programmed a certain way. And a lot of stuff does not serve me any longer. So I know that if I consistently take an emotion, fear of whatever, tie a new behavior to it, the experience is going to be different. Right, and we know the brain is a predicting mechanism. That's where anxiety comes from. You're probably thinking, "What is he going to say next?" That's just what it does, it predicts. But the more I'm able to put new experiences into that predictive pond per se, the whole equation changes. >> Which I think is really a an a really positive, powerful message for people uh because it it it's first of all physiological and it opens up the idea that you don't have to remain uh underperforming or trapped or repeat things that are that are sort of making you unhappy. And I I know about some of this from from looking into anxiety and it's it's it's amazing what the brain is able to to So I think I think it's a really positive message. One thing we hear a lot, so I'm going to give you some examples from from feedback that we get from people in our community and questions that come up a lot, and I think there's an undercurrent in them. So, one of the things uh that people be very frustrated about is that they did a lot of research on a on a sector or on a company, and they felt really confident about that, and they felt like all of that analysis should have resulted in success. Is that enough, or are there other things that we need to be aware of in addition to sort of that sort of, you know, the homework, putting in the time and the homework? >> So, we always talk about best decisions. You don't know if they're right, only time tells you if you're right. So, but one best decisions doesn't mean you're going to have a best outcome. It just doesn't. We could have the most data we have, and everything, and we could be wrong. But now you start coupling best decisions together, I can't guarantee you a monetary outcome of how much, but the more repetition you're able to do that, the outcome in in in probability should end up in your favor. The question relates to like so, I have all this data, external data, right? I've done all this work. I'll give you an example. You and I have the same investment pieces. Same trading pieces. We have an a trading idea. I lose a lot of money, and you make a lot of money. Why is that? We have the same data. >> [clears throat] >> It all becomes little the mental edge is everything. So, when you when somebody says, "I did all this work, and it didn't work." Okay, you're dealing with uncertainty. Now, now you're talking time duration. Were you not right for the day? You know, does it play out 6 months from now? What do What do you How are you thinking about this idea? But more importantly, you know, I hate when people say this, by the way. Every trade investment is an independent event. I think you've heard this before. Everything is independent. And that's not true. Intellectually, it should be independent. But our nervous systems carry residue from each decision we make. Just knowing that is powerful. Now, how do you clear your nervous system in order to go to almost a zero-zero mindset and to be able to attack again. To make a best decision again. Because a lot of people don't realize that. It's like when you know, you heard people you can't multi you know, you can multitask all you want, but you can't multi-focus. There's always residue in each decision we make. So, when you think about mental performance or decision-making in general, how do I clear that up? To give myself the best odds. Is it disappointing that I've done all this work and it didn't work? Yeah. Is it frustrating? Yes. Is it annoying? Yes. Welcome to the game. But really, how do you recalibrate to come back again? That is one of the most important things to learn right there. It's not personal. We do take things personal because now we feel embarrassed, we feel judged. All these fears start to come in. Give you some Give yourself permission to feel that. But going back to the beginning of this conversation, we have the ability to choose conscious behavior again and again and again. You know, when you look at the markets today, I call this adult swimming. You know, people were like, "Hey, how would you be doing?" I said, "Well, I'm glad I'm on the other side." >> Right? It's hard. >> Yeah, but I lived through, you know, Asian Contagion. I lived through the dot com, I've lived through, obviously, '08. You know, a little bit of COVID. It's a very challenging profession. And to have the ability to know how to reset, down regulate, create space, to go on to make another decision, is invaluable. But we think that it just happens out of nowhere. This is really what mental performance training is. And that's why the best stay the best. >> I feel like people feel that sort of sense of fatigue and are stressed out. I want to ask you about something I I saw you talking about, which I think speaks to this, uh, recognizing this residue we carry with us, or or the sort of things that are happening underneath surface that we may not be aware of. Um, and you talk about anchoring bias. Can you talk a little bit about what that is, and like why that gets in the way of decisions that we make? >> Well, I I don't talk about anchoring bias per se, but what I do talk about is patterns. So, going going with you what you just said, the markets are ever changing. Right? Nobody I mean, you know, when I started I I didn't think the internet would work. Swear to god. I'm like, what's sending email? >> [laughter] >> Right? But the question is, are you able to really adapt? It's it's a constant adaptation game. >> Mhm. >> And the minute you think you know what you're doing, and that's not a strike against your confidence. I think confidence is knowing that you don't know everything. And having the ability to be curious in order to expand. So, when you have an anchoring bias, you are shutting yourself off from a lot of things. >> Right. You're referencing you're referencing something that happened to you once as the sort of point that you keep coming back to. And I'll And I'll throw something out in real time that I think is Um, we hear a lot people saying, "I missed it. I could have sold." As we've had we have had a big move in gold. A lot of people in this community own gold. I I it it it went all the way up and now it's down 20%. I missed my chance. It's never going to go back up and I should have sold. Or I missed the opportunity to ride the bull market. It happened and I'm left out because everyone's just focusing on that high that was just reached, not anything else that happened. And that strikes me as something that that sounds very similar to um what you talk about. >> You know, it's it's so interesting that when you talk about gold, right? We're talking about a couple months ago when gold and silver went bananas. >> Yeah. >> It's it's All right, so you own gold forever and it finally moves. Right? And it goes up 50% or 40% you make a tick. And then it goes higher and you feel like an idiot. >> Right. >> We don't want to feel a certain way. Right? I I I've met many people who've top-ticked things in my life. So, the real question has to be like, well, how will you feel if you don't be a if you're not a part of this? Well, I'm I'm going to have a lot of regret. All right, so if that is true, how do you want to structure this trade? Well, I think I should own a little bit. This goes back to questions. I'm going to sell some as Buffett said, maybe it's your worst sell ever. And it goes higher. Nobody can time these things, but we take it so personally. Like I was in silver since '08. And I think I sold most of my position at 92. And then it obviously went much higher. Now, do I feel good? Dude, I can't believe this thing's trading 92. But for me, it's like, well done, Ev. I've no idea where this thing is going. How do I down regulate? Right? I can kick a little dirt if I want cuz it went to 130, whatever it did. All right, what's your next move? >> Mhm. >> Are we going to stay here and ruminate the what is the could is and the should is, all the cousins? That's not healthy. And is that going to really take me out of my decision-making game? I don't want to be there. So, then I got to train myself to accept, create space, and say to myself, "What is the next best idea you have?" >> Mhm. >> Now, is that easy? No. But, it gets easier. >> Yeah, it sounds like awareness is a really important part of this. Uh recognizing the trigger of your of your patterns, I guess. >> But, you know you know what's interesting when people come to me, whatever whether it's athletics, business, or or or or markets, they always think it's about the leaf on a tree. They come they think it's about something and it's never about that. It's always about your roots. >> Mhm. >> It's always about the the mental makeup of what's going on between your ears. Always. And that's my job as a coach to go figure that out. Because we do repeat patterns and we think it's something else and it's never what you think it is. So, for your listeners, to know that 90% of this is mental. >> Mhm. >> That alone should be freeing. Saying that I'm going to have better control over how I behave, not how I feel. Can't control emotions, right? Behavior changes before feelings. So, I'm going to start and it doesn't have to be a Herculean move. I'm going to start changing certain things of how I respond to things. And then you start going down that down that sequence of events. It'll make you much stronger decision maker. And remember, we're not always going to be right. You know, when people talk about and this drives me nuts if I can share this with you. When people talk about win-loss ratios, I'm like I my best year my win-loss ratio was 20%. I go, "But, my slugging ratio was massive." So, I'm allowed to be wrong. I just got to know how to manage money. >> I think it's inevitable that we're wrong. >> It's inevitable, but the question is do you is that a score against you? >> The other thing I want to end on is about learning and success. That we the idea that we learn when we've have trials and tribulations, but this idea of learning in success is something we don't think about cuz if you're successful, you're doing it right. You don't need any any of this. >> Think of it this way, Maggie. When you're successful, you are constantly putting yourself in uncharted territories. >> Mhm. >> That's what expansion is. So, if that is true and when my people do well, how do we recommit? Not not take for granted the basics that got us there, but actually leverage that. So, it's it's when people are doing well, you know, we call it recommit time. All right, time to recommit. >> Mhm. >> Let's go over all the things we know. Where do we need to get better? And these are incredible, you know, who have done extremely well, but if something's going outside the way I want to know about it. Right? Let's make sure, you know, I don't believe in work-life balance, but I do believe in work-life harmony. Where we're able to be in rhythm with ourselves and we know life's not linear. I'm not saying that. But when we are in a positions of pressure and I think, you know, like Billie Jean King says and I say it a lot, pressure is a privilege. >> Mhm. >> How do we handle that? Well, I think to know that you're not there yet is a wonderful place to be. Like I'm 54 years old and I had a decent career on Wall Street, pretty decent. And now I'm I'm building an M1 Performance Group. I did a TEDx at 54. I didn't even know I'd do that. I'm doing more speeches. I got to I'd serve some of the greatest people in the world. I'm not even close. My kids and my brother's an entrepreneur also. He owns a bunch of restaurants. My kids are like, "Why do you do all this?" I said, "I'm not there yet. I'm in the second inning of this thing." >> Yeah. >> They go, "Why?" I go, "Cuz you know, as Nick Saban says, there's a capability gap. I want to go experience these things." >> Yeah. >> And I know it's not linear. So, I'm going to feel like everybody else feels. I live this human experience like everybody else does. But why not? >> Yeah. I think it's I think it's great stuff and I think it's such a such a good time. You know, we've got a little sort of gap here and there's so much going on. I think it's a great time to kind of reset. People are taking some time on vacation, maybe taking time to unplug a little. So, it's a fantastic time to sort of think about this other side of investing and performing and trying to strive to do better. Uh and maybe we can all get Have a such a pleasure. >> Yeah, you know, for people just just real quick like getting into your body. When when there's so much stuff going on, you can't overthink overthinking. Get into your body. Go for a run. Just go train. Create some space so you can just think. Cuz you're right, there's a lot of stuff going on. Give yourself the ability to succeed and stay successful. >> I love it. >> take that for granted. >> I love it. Evan, thank you so much. I feel energized. Thank you for taking the time. >> it. I'm a New Yorker with a lot of I'm not a motivational speaker, but I speak with a lot of passion. >> We all We all do. It's in the It's in the water. >> [laughter] >> It's in the water. >> Thank you so much, Evan. >> Thank you for having me.
